sections.title: Harassment
sections.summary: Section 26 defines THREE types of harassment: (1) s.26(1): unwanted conduct RELATED TO a relevant protected characteristic which has the purpose or effect of violating the victim's dignity, or creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment; (2) s.26(2): SEXUAL HARASSMENT — unwanted conduct of a SEXUAL NATURE with the same purpose or effect; (3) s.26(3): less favourable treatment because of the victim's rejection of or submission to unwanted conduct of a sexual nature or related to sex/gender reassignment. The protected characteristics covered are: age, disability, gender reassignment, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Marriage/civil partnership and pregnancy/maternity are NOT covered by s.26 (but may be covered by direct discrimination under s.13/s.18). The three-part 'effect' test (s.26(4)) considers: (a) the perception of the victim; (b) the other circumstances; (c) whether it is reasonable for the conduct to have that effect.
sections.text:
(1) A person (A) harasses another (B) if—
(a) A engages in unwanted conduct related to a relevant protected characteristic, and
(b) the conduct has the purpose or effect of—
(i) violating B's dignity, or
(ii) creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment for B.

(2) A also harasses B if—
(a) A engages in unwanted conduct of a sexual nature, and
(b) the conduct has the purpose or effect referred to in subsection (1)(b).

(3) A also harasses B if—
(a) A or another person engages in unwanted conduct of a sexual nature or that is related to gender reassignment or sex,
(b) the conduct has the purpose or effect referred to in subsection (1)(b), and
(c) because of B's rejection of or submission to the conduct, A treats B less favourably than A would treat B if B had not rejected or submitted to the conduct.

(4) In deciding whether conduct has the effect referred to in subsection (1)(b), each of the following must be taken into account—
(a) the perception of B,
(b) the other circumstances of the case,
(c) whether it is reasonable for the conduct to have that effect.

(5) The relevant protected characteristics are—
age; disability; gender reassignment; race; religion or belief; sex; sexual orientation.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 26 creates a STANDALONE cause of action for harassment — separate from direct discrimination (s.13) and victimisation (s.27). The critical distinction between harassment and discrimination is that harassment does not require a comparator. The conduct must be 'related to' the protected characteristic — not 'because of' it. This broader test means conduct that is ASSOCIATED with a protected characteristic (even if not directed at the victim personally) can constitute harassment. The three-part 'effect' test balances the subjective perception of the victim against an objective reasonableness standard — preventing hypersensitive claims while protecting genuine victims.

type1_relatedConduct.description: Unwanted conduct RELATED TO a relevant protected characteristic
type1_relatedConduct.examples: Racial jokes in the workplace, homophobic comments, ageist remarks, displaying offensive material, use of slurs

type2_sexualHarassment.description: Unwanted conduct of a SEXUAL NATURE
type2_sexualHarassment.examples: Sexual comments, sexual jokes, unwanted touching, sexual propositions, displaying sexual material, intrusive questions about sex life
type2_sexualHarassment.note: This is the type covered by the s.40A preventative duty (Worker Protection Act 2023)

type3_lessFavourableTreatment.description: Less favourable treatment because of rejection of or submission to sexual conduct or conduct related to sex/gender reassignment
type3_lessFavourableTreatment.examples: Employee rejected supervisor's advances and was then passed over for promotion; employee submitted to harassment and was labelled as 'easy'

threeFactors: (a) The perception of B (subjective — did the victim feel their dignity was violated?)
(b) The other circumstances of the case (objective context — nature of the conduct, relationship between parties, workplace culture)
(c) Whether it is REASONABLE for the conduct to have that effect (objective check — prevents hypersensitive claims)
effectTest.practicalNote: The 'purpose or effect' test is disjunctive: if the conduct has the PURPOSE of harassing, harassment is established regardless of effect. If the conduct has the EFFECT (but not purpose) of harassing, the tribunal must apply the three-part test in s.26(4). This means intentional harassment is easier to prove than unintentional harassment.

relatedToNotBecauseOf.principle: 'Related to' (s.26(1)) is a BROADER test than 'because of' (s.13 direct discrimination). Conduct does not need to be directed AT the victim personally, nor does it need to be motivated by the protected characteristic. It is sufficient that the conduct is ASSOCIATED with the characteristic. A heterosexual employee who is subjected to homophobic banter may have a harassment claim — the conduct is related to sexual orientation even though the victim is not gay.

versions.amendedBy: Equality Act 2010 as enacted — s.26 is substantively unchanged

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not amend s.26 directly. However: (1) s.43B(1)(da) ERA 1996 makes sexual harassment a qualifying disclosure for whistleblowing from 6 April 2026; (2) ERA 2025 strengthens the s.40A preventative duty and reintroduces third-party harassment liability (both expected October 2026). These changes operate through s.40, not s.26 — but they significantly affect the practical framework around harassment.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — the combination of the preventative duty, third-party liability, and whistleblowing protection for sexual harassment disclosures creates a comprehensive enforcement framework.

refs: EqA 2010 s.40 (employer duty not to harass)
EqA 2010 s.40A (preventative duty — reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment)
EqA 2010 s.120(8) (25% uplift for s.40A breach)
ERA 1996 s.43B(1)(da) (sexual harassment as qualifying disclosure — from 6 April 2026)

leadingCases.name: Richmond Pharmacology v Dhaliwal
leadingCases.principle: The three-part effect test in s.26(4) prevents hypersensitive claims. The tribunal must consider whether it was REASONABLE for the conduct to have the effect of violating dignity or creating a hostile environment. A single remark that causes offence but would not reasonably be regarded as harassment may not satisfy the test.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that a single remark about the claimant's ethnic origin, while unwanted and related to race, did not satisfy the s.26(4) reasonableness test. The tribunal must guard against trivialisation of the concept of harassment.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational EAT authority on the s.26(4) reasonableness check. Prevents trivial or oversensitive claims from succeeding — the conduct must objectively warrant the label 'harassment'.

leadingCases.name: Pemberton v Inwood
leadingCases.principle: The 'related to' test in s.26(1) is broad — it does not require the conduct to be motivated by the protected characteristic. Conduct that is associated with or connected to the characteristic is sufficient. However, there must be a genuine connection — not merely a coincidental link.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that 'related to' is a broad factual inquiry. The tribunal must consider all the circumstances to determine whether the conduct was related to the protected characteristic.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the breadth of 'related to' while setting the boundary — there must be a genuine connection, not speculation.

leadingCases.name: Land Registry v Grant
leadingCases.principle: The concepts of 'violating dignity' and 'creating an offensive environment' are distinct and should not be conflated. 'Violating dignity' involves a high threshold — conduct that is deeply offensive and undermines the person's sense of self. 'Creating an offensive environment' is a lower threshold but must still be assessed against the s.26(4) reasonableness standard.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that tribunals should not trivialise the concept of violating dignity by applying it to every instance of workplace rudeness or insensitivity.
leadingCases.significance: Distinguishes between the two limbs of s.26(1)(b) and emphasises that 'violating dignity' is a high bar.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Sexual harassment and harassment at work: technical guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: THE authoritative EHRC technical guidance on s.26 harassment — includes examples, the effect test, and employer obligations.

commonErrors: Confusing harassment (s.26) with direct discrimination (s.13) — harassment does NOT require a comparator. The claimant does not need to show they were treated less favourably than someone without the protected characteristic. The test is whether the conduct was unwanted, related to the characteristic, and had the purpose or effect of violating dignity or creating a hostile environment

Not recognising the THREE types of harassment — s.26 defines three distinct types: (1) unwanted conduct related to a protected characteristic; (2) sexual harassment (conduct of a sexual nature); (3) less favourable treatment for rejecting/submitting. Each has different elements and an employer may face claims under more than one type simultaneously

Applying the 'purpose or effect' test incorrectly — if the conduct has the PURPOSE of harassing, harassment is established WITHOUT applying the s.26(4) test. The three-part reasonableness test in s.26(4) only applies where the claim is based on EFFECT (not purpose). Intentional harassment needs no reasonableness check

Not understanding the s.26(4) three-part effect test — the tribunal must consider: (a) the victim's perception (subjective); (b) the other circumstances (objective context); (c) whether it is REASONABLE for the conduct to have the effect (objective check). All three must be considered — a claim cannot succeed on the victim's subjective perception alone if it is not objectively reasonable (Richmond Pharmacology v Dhaliwal [2009])

Assuming 'related to' means 'because of' — s.26(1) uses 'related to' which is BROADER than 'because of' (used in s.13). Conduct does not need to be motivated by the protected characteristic. A heterosexual employee subjected to homophobic banter has a claim — the conduct is related to sexual orientation even though they do not share the characteristic

Not recognising that pregnancy and maternity are NOT covered by s.26 — the protected characteristics for s.26 are: age, disability, gender reassignment, race, religion or belief, sex, sexual orientation. Marriage/civil partnership and pregnancy/maternity are excluded. Harassment related to pregnancy may be claimed as direct discrimination under s.18 instead

Treating a single incident as automatically insufficient — a SINGLE act can constitute harassment if it is sufficiently serious. There is no requirement for a course of conduct or repeated behaviour. A single racial slur, a single sexual assault, or a single grossly offensive remark can meet the s.26 threshold

Not understanding the 'sexual nature' element of s.26(2) — sexual harassment requires conduct 'of a sexual nature'. This includes: sexual comments, jokes, gestures, touching, propositions, displaying sexual material, intrusive questions about sex life, and sexual messages. It does not cover conduct related to SEX (the protected characteristic) that is not sexual in nature — that falls under s.26(1)

Conflating harassment with banter — workplace 'banter' that is related to a protected characteristic and is unwanted can constitute harassment. The fact that it was intended as humour or that others participated willingly does not prevent it from being unwanted by the victim. The test is subjective (from the victim's perspective) checked against objective reasonableness

Not recognising that harassment compensation is UNCAPPED — EqA 2010 compensation for harassment is uncapped and includes: injury to feelings (Vento bands), financial loss, aggravated damages in appropriate cases, and interest. There is no statutory cap equivalent to the unfair dismissal compensatory award

Not recognising the whistleblowing interaction — from 6 April 2026, sexual harassment is a qualifying disclosure under s.43B(1)(da) ERA 1996. A worker who reports sexual harassment and suffers detriment has BOTH an EqA 2010 harassment/victimisation claim AND an ERA 1996 whistleblowing detriment claim (s.47B). The two frameworks now overlap explicitly

sections.title: Employer's duty — harassment and preventative duty
sections.summary: s.40(1): An employer must not harass an employee. s.40A (inserted by Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023, in force 26 October 2024): An employer MUST take REASONABLE STEPS to prevent SEXUAL HARASSMENT of its employees in the course of employment. This is a PREVENTATIVE, ANTICIPATORY duty — the employer must proactively assess risks and take steps BEFORE harassment occurs. Breach: EHRC enforcement action + 25% uplift on compensation if harassment claim succeeds. ERA 2025 (expected October 2026): (1) strengthens the duty from 'reasonable steps' to 'ALL reasonable steps'; (2) reintroduces THIRD-PARTY harassment liability — employer must take all reasonable steps to prevent third parties (customers, clients, visitors) from harassing employees (applies to ALL protected characteristics, not just sexual harassment). Free expression protection applies for non-sexual harassment — employer not expected to prevent expression of opinions on political/moral/religious/social matters.
sections.text:
Section 40:
(1) An employer (A) must not, in relation to employment by A, harass a person (B)—
(a) who is an employee of A's;
(b) who has applied to A for employment.

Section 40A:
(1) An employer (A) must take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of each of A's employees (B) in the course of B's employment.
(2) For the purposes of this section, 'sexual harassment' means harassment within section 26(2).
sections.keyPrinciple: The s.40A preventative duty represents a fundamental shift from REACTIVE to PROACTIVE employer obligations. Before s.40A, employers were liable for harassment that occurred (s.40(1) + s.109 vicarious liability). Under s.40A, employers must take steps to PREVENT harassment from occurring in the first place — this is an anticipatory duty that applies regardless of whether any harassment has actually occurred. The EHRC technical guidance states that an employer is unlikely to comply with the duty unless it carries out a risk assessment. ERA 2025 will strengthen this further: 'reasonable steps' becomes 'ALL reasonable steps', and third-party liability is reintroduced.

preventativeDuty.scope: SEXUAL HARASSMENT only (s.26(2)) — not general harassment related to protected characteristics
preventativeDuty.standard: Reasonable steps to PREVENT — anticipatory and proactive
preventativeDuty.breach: EHRC enforcement action (investigation, unlawful act notice, action plan, court order) + 25% uplift on compensation if employee succeeds in a harassment claim (s.120(8))
preventativeDuty.riskAssessment: EHRC guidance states an employer is 'unlikely to be able to comply' without carrying out a risk assessment identifying where and how sexual harassment could occur
practicalSteps: Training for all staff including managers
Prompt and thorough investigation of complaints
Specific measures for high-risk environments (hospitality, customer-facing, events)

allReasonableSteps.change: ERA 2025 strengthens the s.40A duty from 'reasonable steps' to 'ALL reasonable steps' — expected October 2026
allReasonableSteps.significance: The word 'all' raises the bar. The employer must demonstrate it took EVERY reasonable step — not just some. A policy alone will not suffice. The employer must show active, comprehensive implementation.

thirdPartyHarassment.change: ERA 2025 reintroduces employer liability for THIRD-PARTY harassment (new s.40(1A)) — expected October 2026
thirdPartyHarassment.scope: Applies to ALL protected characteristics covered by s.26(1), not just sexual harassment
thirdPartyHarassment.thirdParties: Customers, clients, patients, visitors, contractors, suppliers, members of the public
thirdPartyHarassment.standard: Employer must take 'all reasonable steps' to prevent the third party from harassing the employee
thirdPartyHarassment.freeExpressionProtection: New subsections (1C)/(1D) and (4A)/(4B) provide that, in certain circumstances, the employer is NOT required to prevent the expression of opinions on political, moral, religious or social matters — but this protection does NOT apply to sexual harassment

vicarousLiability.principle: The employer is vicariously liable for harassment by its employees in the course of employment — UNLESS the employer took ALL reasonable steps to prevent the harassment. This is the existing 'statutory defence' (s.109(4)). It applies separately from the s.40A preventative duty. An employer who successfully defends vicarious liability under s.109(4) may still breach the s.40A preventative duty.
vicarousLiability.contrast: s.109 vicarious liability = liability for harassment that DID occur (reactive). s.40A preventative duty = duty to prevent harassment (proactive). Both can apply to the same facts — but they are separate obligations.

versions.amendedBy: EqA 2010 s.40 as enacted; s.40A inserted by Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023, in force 26 October 2024

pendingAmendment.effect: (1) 'Reasonable steps' → 'ALL reasonable steps' (expected October 2026); (2) Third-party harassment liability reintroduced (expected October 2026); (3) Regulations specifying what constitutes 'reasonable steps' — consultation expected 2027/2028.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: October 2026 (both changes confirmed in Government roadmap).
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — fundamentally reshapes employer harassment obligations. The combination of 'all reasonable steps' + third-party liability + whistleblowing protection creates the most comprehensive anti-harassment framework in UK law.

refs: EqA 2010 s.40 (employer duty not to harass)
EqA 2010 s.40A (preventative duty — in force 26 October 2024)
EqA 2010 s.26 (definition of harassment — three types)
EqA 2010 s.109 (vicarious liability for employee acts)
EqA 2010 s.120(8) (25% uplift for s.40A breach)
Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023 (inserted s.40A)
ERA 2025 (strengthens to 'all reasonable steps' + reintroduces third-party liability — October 2026)
EHRC Technical Guidance on Sexual Harassment (September 2024)

leadingCases.name: Bessong v Pennine Care NHS Foundation Trust
leadingCases.principle: The s.109(4) 'all reasonable steps' defence requires the employer to show it took all steps that were reasonably practicable to prevent the harassment. A harassment policy is necessary but not sufficient — the employer must show active implementation, training, monitoring, and enforcement.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that having a policy is not enough. The employer must demonstrate that the policy was communicated, that training was delivered, that complaints were investigated, and that the policy was actively enforced.
leadingCases.significance: Sets the standard for the 'all reasonable steps' defence — and by extension, for the s.40A preventative duty. A paper policy is insufficient.

leadingCases.name: Unite the Union v Nailard
leadingCases.principle: Following the repeal of s.40(2)–(4) in 2013, employers were NOT vicariously liable for third-party harassment under s.26. The remaining provisions of s.26 could not be read as imposing third-party liability. This gap is being filled by ERA 2025's reintroduction of third-party harassment liability.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the 2013 repeal of s.40(2)–(4) removed third-party harassment liability entirely. The remaining s.26 provisions could not be stretched to cover it.
leadingCases.significance: THE authority on the third-party harassment gap created by the 2013 repeal. This gap is being closed by ERA 2025 (expected October 2026).

leadingCases.name: Allay (UK) Ltd v Gehlen
leadingCases.principle: Stale or ineffective training does not satisfy the 'all reasonable steps' defence. Training must be refreshed regularly to remain effective. Training delivered years earlier that has not prevented further harassment is evidence that the training was inadequate.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employer's equality and diversity training, delivered over 18 months earlier, had become 'stale' and was demonstrably ineffective given that further harassment had occurred. The s.109(4) defence failed.
leadingCases.significance: THE key authority on training quality under the 'all reasonable steps' standard. Training must be regular, refreshed, and demonstrably effective. Stale training = failed defence.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employer 8-step guide: Preventing sexual harassment at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: THE authoritative EHRC guidance on the s.40A preventative duty — 8-step employer guide including risk assessment, policy, training, and monitoring.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Sexual harassment
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on preventing and dealing with sexual harassment at work.

commonErrors: Not recognising that the s.40A preventative duty is already IN FORCE — the Worker Protection Act 2023 came into force on 26 October 2024. Employers are ALREADY under a duty to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment. This is not a future obligation — it is current law

Treating a harassment policy as sufficient compliance — having a policy is NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT. The employer must demonstrate: risk assessment, active training (not stale), clear reporting channels, prompt investigation, monitoring, and enforcement (Bessong v Pennine [2020]; Allay v Gehlen [2021])

Not understanding the 25% uplift for s.40A breach — if an employee succeeds in a sexual harassment claim AND the employer breached the s.40A preventative duty, the tribunal can uplift compensation by up to 25% (s.120(8)). This is a significant financial penalty on top of the uncapped harassment compensation

Confusing the current 'reasonable steps' with the forthcoming 'ALL reasonable steps' — the current s.40A standard is 'reasonable steps'. ERA 2025 strengthens this to 'ALL reasonable steps' from October 2026. The word 'all' raises the bar — the employer must take EVERY reasonable step, not just some

Not preparing for third-party harassment liability — ERA 2025 reintroduces employer liability for third-party harassment (expected October 2026). This applies to ALL protected characteristics, not just sexual harassment. Employers in hospitality, retail, healthcare, and other customer-facing sectors face particular exposure

Assuming the free expression protection allows all opinions — the free expression provisions (new s.40(1C)/(1D) and (4A)/(4B)) only protect the expression of opinions on political, moral, religious, or social matters in NON-SEXUAL harassment contexts. The protection does NOT apply to sexual harassment. And it does not protect conduct that has the PURPOSE (as opposed to effect) of violating dignity

Not recognising the gap between the 2013 repeal and ERA 2025 — from October 2013 to October 2026 (approximately), there was NO employer liability for third-party harassment (Unite v Nailard [2019] EWCA confirmed the gap). ERA 2025 closes this gap. Employers must prepare NOW for the October 2026 commencement

Not carrying out a risk assessment — EHRC guidance states an employer is 'unlikely to be able to comply with the preventative duty unless they carry out a risk assessment.' The risk assessment should identify where and how sexual harassment could occur (high-risk settings, events, customer interactions, lone working, etc.) and what steps can be taken to prevent it

Relying on stale training — training delivered years ago that has not been refreshed is demonstrably ineffective and will not satisfy the 'all reasonable steps' standard (Allay v Gehlen [2021]). Training must be regular (at least annual), refreshed after incidents, and tailored to the workplace context

Not understanding the interaction with whistleblowing — from 6 April 2026, sexual harassment is a qualifying disclosure under s.43B(1)(da) ERA 1996. A worker who reports sexual harassment and suffers detriment has BOTH an EqA 2010 harassment claim AND an ERA 1996 whistleblowing detriment claim (s.47B). Employers face exposure on two fronts

Not distinguishing between s.109 vicarious liability and s.40A preventative duty — s.109 = employer is vicariously liable for employee harassment UNLESS 'all reasonable steps' defence. s.40A = separate duty to take steps to PREVENT harassment (regardless of whether any harassment has occurred). An employer can defend s.109 successfully but still breach s.40A — they are separate obligations
